event: The Tribute Money — Render unto Caesar
symbols: coin or denarius — the tension between earthly and heavenly authority, Caesar's image versus God's image in man
Christ's upward-pointing hand — directing attention from earthly concerns to divine authority and heavenly kingdom
group of questioners pressing close — the trap set by religious leaders, the hostility of the world toward Christ
Christ in red and blue garments — dual nature of Christ as both divine (blue/heavenly) and sacrificial (red/blood)
description: A dark interior scene showing Christ at right, wearing a red tunic and blue mantle, His right arm raised with finger pointing upward. He gazes steadily at a group of men who press close from the left side. An older bald man in the foreground extends his hand holding what appears to be a small coin or paper. Behind him, several men in turbans and varied robes crowd together, their faces expressing suspicion and intent scrutiny. The lighting dramatically illuminates Christ's face and the hands exchanging the coin while the background recedes into shadow.
theological_description: The Tribute Money episode (Matthew 22:15-22) is one of the most significant confrontations in Jesus' ministry, where the Pharisees attempt to trap Him between sedition against Rome and compromise of divine authority. When shown the denarius bearing Caesar's image, Jesus' response — 'Render unto Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and unto God the things that are God's' — astonished His questioners and established a foundational principle of Christian political theology. Christ's upward-pointing finger directs attention beyond the temporal dispute to the ultimate sovereignty of God. The coin bears Caesar's image (eikon), but humanity itself bears God's image (Genesis 1:27) — the deeper point is that while coins belong to Caesar, human beings belong wholly to God. Church Fathers like Tertullian and Augustine drew from this passage the theology of the two cities and the Christian's dual citizenship. The painting captures the dramatic tension of the moment: the conspirators' intensity versus Christ's composed authority.

scripture_refs.reason: The Pharisees ask about paying taxes to Caesar and Jesus responds with the coin — the direct scene depicted
Mark's parallel account of the tribute money incident with Pharisees and Herodians
Paul's teaching on submission to governing authorities and paying taxes — extending Christ's teaching
God created man in His own image — the deeper meaning: Caesar's image is on the coin, God's image is on man
Luke's account specifying the spies sent to trap Jesus in His words before the governor
